== Mythical narrative ==
{{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}}
Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>

{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}}  were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought  them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />

She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>

During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:

{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}

This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.

==Genealogy==
{{Generations of Jimmu|draft}}

{{Expand Japanese|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} {{Infobox royalty | title = {{ill|Saigū (priestess)|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigū (priestess)|qq=}} | image = File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px | caption = {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|lt=Sakurai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.|successor = {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}| | house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial House of Japan|qq=}}|father = {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
{{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命}} was a Japanese mythological figure and {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|WD=Q863048|lt=princess}}<ref>{{Cite web |last=KansaiOdyssey |date=2017-07-26 |title=Oh Gods! The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.

{{Expand Japanese|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} {{Infobox royalty | title = {{ill|Saigū (priestess)|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigū (priestess)|qq=}} | image = File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px | caption = {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|lt=Sakurai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.|successor = {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}| | house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial House of Japan|qq=}}|father = {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
{{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命}} was a Japanese mythological figure and {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|WD=Q863048|lt=princess}}<ref>{{Cite web |last=KansaiOdyssey |date=2017-07-26 |title=Oh Gods! The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.
==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu|draft}} == See Also ==

The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.

{{Infobox royalty | title = {{ill|Saigū (priestess)|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigū (priestess)|qq=}} | image = File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px | caption = {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|lt=Sakurai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.|successor = {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}| | house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial House of Japan|qq=}}|father = {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
{{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命}} was a Japanese mythological figure and {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|WD=Q863048|lt=princess}}<ref>{{Cite web |last=KansaiOdyssey |date=2017-07-26 |title=Oh Gods! The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.
==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu|draft}} == See Also ==
* {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Miwa clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|qq=}} ==Notes== {{notelist|qq=draft}}

It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.
==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu|draft}} == See Also ==

{{Infobox royalty | title = {{ill|Saigū (priestess)|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigū (priestess)|qq=}} | image = File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px | caption = {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|lt=Sakurai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.|successor = {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}| | house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial House of Japan|qq=}}|father = {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
{{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命}} was a Japanese mythological figure and {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|WD=Q863048|lt=princess|1=Princess}}<ref>{{Cite web |last=KansaiOdyssey |date=2017-07-26 |title=Oh Gods! The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.

The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.

The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.

The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.
==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu|draft}} == See Also ==
* {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Miwa clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|qq=}} ==Notes== {{notelist|qq=draft}}

The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. {{Infobox royalty | title = {{ill|Saigū (priestess)|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigū (priestess)|qq=}} | image = File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px | caption = {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|lt=Sakurai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.|successor = {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}| | house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial House of Japan|qq=}}|father = {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} }}
{{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命}} was a Japanese mythological figure and {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|WD=Q863048|lt=princess|1=Princess}}<ref>{{Cite web |last=KansaiOdyssey |date=2017-07-26 |title=Oh Gods! The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref>. She was the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|lt=Saigū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022" />
Her brother was the similarly named {{ill|Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokihiribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}
== Mythical narrative == {{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}} Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>
{{ill|Tatari|lt=Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatari|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|lt=Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}} were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Sujin placed his daughter {{Nihongo|{{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref name="Saigū|國學院大學デジタルミュージアム-2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-05-22 |title=Saigū {{!|qq=draft}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |access-date=2023-11-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522022806/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 |archive-date=2022-05-22}}</ref>entrusting with her the {{ill|Yata no Kagami|lt=mirror|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yata no Kagami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qq=}}, she brought them to the {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}}.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" />
She moved across many {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} throughout her life.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref>
During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|lt=Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|lt=Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]:
{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}
This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.
==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu|draft}} == See Also ==
* {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Miwa clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|qq=}} ==Notes== {{notelist|qq=draft}}

Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>

It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso.
